coredata.dc:title: Human fingernails as an intriguing precursor for the synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon dots with strong fluorescent properties: Analytical and bioimaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Chatzimitakos, Theodoros
Kasouni, Athanasia
Sygellou, Lamprini
Leonardos, Ioannis
Troganis, Anastasios
Stalikas, Constantine
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we describe the synthesis of N,S co-doped bright carbon dots (CDs) from human fingernails via a simple microwave irradiation procedure. Human fingernails are utilized to prepare a new kind of fluorescent CDs after sulfuric acid-enhanced microwave treatment, which show good stability in aqueous solutions, high quantum yield as well as remarkable features for fluorescence quenching-based detection and cell imaging. The procedure followed for the synthesis is very fast and energy-efficient, enabling the low-cost production of CDs with distinctive properties. The as-synthesized CDs can be used as a selective, efficient and sensitive fluorescent probe for the detection of sunset yellow in food products. Ultra-low limit of detection (0.1 nM), linear response between 0.3 and 43.4 nM and splendid reproducibility for the fluorescence-based detection of sunset yellow are the highlights of the developed analytical method. The interaction mechanism between CDs and sunset yellow is associated both with inner filter effect and static quenching. Furthermore, the produced CDs do not affect cell viability, rendering them suitable for bioimaging purposes. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that human fingernails, a natural source of high abundance, is used as raw material for synthetic, probing and bioimaging purposes, after microwave-assisted treatment, in the presence of sulfuric acid.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Human fingernails
Carbon dots
Sunset yellow fluorescent probing
Static quenching
Inner filter effect
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent probing of sunset yellow and selectivity study

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Deconvoluted XPS peaks of C1s (a), O1 s (b), N1s (c) and S2p for the as-prepared CDs.
(A) Emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths and (B) excitation spectrum at emission of 380 nm of the CDs.
Calibration curve for the detection of SY; Inset: linear range of the calibration curve.
Fluorescence microscopy images of (A) non-treated and CDs-treated (B) MDA-MB-231, (C) A549, (D) HeLa, under UV (1) and visible (2) light.
Real sample analysis for SY and recoveries at three spiked concentrations.

head.ce:title: Human fingernails as an intriguing precursor for the synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon dots with strong fluorescent properties: Analytical and bioimaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Ioannina, 45110, Ioannina, Greece

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biological Applications and Technology, University of Ioannina, 45110, Ioannina, Greece

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biological Applications and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Foundation for Research and Technology Hellas/Institute of Chemical Engineering Sciences (FORTH/ICE-HT), Stadiou Str., P.O. Box 1414, GR-26504, Rio-Patras, Greece

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Foundation for Research and Technology Hellas/Institute of Chemical Engineering Sciences (FORTH/ICE-HT)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we describe the synthesis of N,S co-doped bright carbon dots (CDs) from human fingernails via a simple microwave irradiation procedure. Human fingernails are utilized to prepare a new kind of fluorescent CDs after sulfuric acid-enhanced microwave treatment, which show good stability in aqueous solutions, high quantum yield as well as remarkable features for fluorescence quenching-based detection and cell imaging. The procedure followed for the synthesis is very fast and energy-efficient, enabling the low-cost production of CDs with distinctive properties. The as-synthesized CDs can be used as a selective, efficient and sensitive fluorescent probe for the detection of sunset yellow in food products. Ultra-low limit of detection (0.1 nM), linear response between 0.3 and 43.4 nM and splendid reproducibility for the fluorescence-based detection of sunset yellow are the highlights of the developed analytical method. The interaction mechanism between CDs and sunset yellow is associated both with inner filter effect and static quenching. Furthermore, the produced CDs do not affect cell viability, rendering them suitable for bioimaging purposes. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that human fingernails, a natural source of high abundance, is used as raw material for synthetic, probing and bioimaging purposes, after microwave-assisted treatment, in the presence of sulfuric acid.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a nascent alternative to semiconductor quantum dots. Their eminence is based on a unique combination of assorted, enthralling properties which derive, mainly, from their minuscule size (referred to also as: zero-dimensional nanomaterials) and surface chemistry. The innovativeness of CDs photoluminescence lies within their surface defects, which serve as surface energy traps and host the radiative recombination of excitons [1,2]. Aside from their captivating fluorescent properties, CDs possess several other compelling properties, such as superb dispersibility in aqueous media, outstanding chemical and colloidal stability, low toxicity and biocompatibility. All these superiorities enable the applicability of CDs in various fields [3].

In analytical chemistry, CDs are used, predominantly, as fluorescent probes for the detection of various compounds [4,5]. Such applications are based on the principle that the interactions between analytes and CDs decrease the fluorescence by quenching. The quenching mechanisms of CDs include static quenching, dynamic quenching, energy transfer, photoinduced electron transfer and inner filter effect [6]. Therefore, it can be inferred that the surface functional groups, greatly affect the aforementioned interactions and as a consequence, the applicability of the CDs. Owing to this, multitudinous approaches have been reported to dope CDs with heteroatoms, such as nitrogen, phosphorus, sulfur, boron and fluorine, either separately or in various combinations. In each case of single heteroatom doping, the merits of the final product differ, while co-doping with two heteroatoms can provide even more improved properties [7]. The nitrogen-sulfur doping appears to be the most appealing and the interest of many researches has focused on it [8]. Despite the efforts made to develop straightforward methods for the synthesis of N,S co-doped CDs, the need for synthesis of high quality CDs with environmentally benign, novel, unsophisticated methods from low-cost sources or even more from natural sources still arises. So far, efforts have been focused on preparing N,S co-doped CDs using various sources of carbon, nitrogen and sulfur, such as amino acids [9], biomass [10], even hair [11].

Synthetic colorants constitute an important class of food additives because they enhance the aesthetic appearance of food products [12]. Sunset yellow (SY), (Fig. 1 ) known also in Europe as E110, is a synthetic azo dye and the second most used food colorant [13]. Due to the azo groups and the aromatic ring structure, it can cause adverse health effects, when consumed extensively [14]. As a result, the use of SY is controlled by laws and regulations in Europe, USA and many other countries, while its use is banned in several others [13]. Since food products are complex matrices, a variety of interferences from other constituents can occur during the identification and quantification of SY. Therefore, new, reliable, alternative methods are needed to overcome this hurdle.

Taking into account that nature provides nearly boundless and easily available sources to replace conventional chemical reagents, in the work herein, the development of a low-cost, straightforward synthesis of bright N,S co-doped CDs from human fingernails is put forward. The precursor, which consists primarily of keratin, an alpha-helical fibrous protein, is natural, abundant and non-toxic and the CDs are produced by rapid microwave heating combined with concentrated sulfuric acid. To showcase their prominence, the produced CDs are utilized as a fluorescent probe for the detection of SY in foodstuffs and beverages and as a cell imaging agent. The strong photoluminescent properties of the resulting CDs, along with the presence of functional groups, make their utilization in certain applications feasible.
All materials are listed in the Supplementary material.

Human fingernails from young, healthy volunteers from the laboratory were collected using cosmetic nail clippers. The nails were rinsed extensively with double distilled water (DDW) and dried with paper towel. Then, 0.50 g of fingernails was brought into a glass beaker, where 5 mL of conc. H2SO4 was added and the mixture was heated in a domestic microwave at 400 W, for 2 min. The dark-colored solution was left to cool down, diluted with 20 mL of DDW under cooling and the pH was adjusted to about 5 using NaOH, in an ice-bath. The solution was filtered through a Whatman filter and after removal of excess salts the concentrated solution was passed through a Sep-Pak C18 cartridge for the removal of residual salts and clean-up of CDs. The dots were eluted with methanol after extensive rinsing of the cartridge with DDW. The removal of the sulfate ions was ensured by the addition of aqueous solution of BaCl2, until no precipitate of BaSO4 was detected. The yellow-colored solution was filtered through 0.20 μm syringe filters (Corning®), freeze-dried and a light-beige residue was obtained, which was stored in dark until use.
Details about the instrumentation for fluorescent probing, characterization and preparation of samples are given in the Supplementary material.
Quantum yield (QY) was calculated by a comparative method (described in the Supplementary material).

The probing procedure for SY determination was as follows: 2 mL of a SY working standard solution or sample solution (diluted 1:20 v/v with water) was mixed with 1 mL of an aqueous solution containing CDs, so that their final concentration was 3 μg mL−1. After vortexing for 30 s, the quenching of the native fluorescence of the CDs was measured at 380 nm, after exciting at 320 nm. Quantification of SY was achieved using a calibration curve of the fluorescence quenching vs the concentration of SY. The selectivity of CDs for SY was evaluated in a similar way by measuring the fluorescence quenching percentage caused by various potential interfering compounds. The tested beverages were analyzed directly, without any pre-treatment. Ice-cream was melted and further analyzed using the aforementioned process.
Information regarding cell growth and cell viability assay are described in the Supplementary material.
Experimental conditions for cellular imaging are provided in the Supplementary material.

Thermal treatment of suitable molecular precursors is an attractive approach for the production of CDs. Expeditious syntheses using microwave radiation have gained considerable attention in recent years, due to the efficient heating of materials using the “MW dielectric heating” effects. The optimal conditions for the synthesis of the CDs were identified via a sequence of experiments, according to which a certain amount of fingernails in sulfuric acid were heated in a microwave oven, at a power of 400–2000 W, for 1 and 2 min. Gradually, the mixture changed from pale yellow to ocher and finally, to dark brown, which signified the production of CDs. In this way, highly fluorescent CDs were produced directly, via a ‘green’, sulfuric acid-assisted, microwave treatment without using surface passivating reagents. The clean-up of CDs was accomplished by passing a concentrated, pH-adjusted solution of them through a Sep-Pak C18 cartridge in order to remove non-fluorescent particles and residual salts.

The optimum as-synthesized product was selected taking into account the maximum fluorescence intensity (Fig. S1 of Supplementary material) and the quenching percentage caused by the same concentration of SY. It was found that the fluorescence spectra of the resulting products were similar and all products had maximum emission at 380–390 nm, when excitation wavelength was set at 320–330 nm (data not shown). In all cases, a 1-min irradiation yielded CDs with fluorescence intensity lower than that of 2 min, because the time taken for CDs formation from organic precursors (e.g. aromatization, nucleation, particle growth etc.) was insufficient in the former case [15]. An irradiation time of 3 min or longer, at any irradiation intensity in the studied range was inappropriate, since it resulted in more extensive decomposition of fingernails, giving rise to the synthesis of products with negligible or no fluorescence [15]. All ten synthesized materials resulting from the combinations of microwave intensity and time were tested for their response in the presence of SY. It was found that fluorescence quenching was almost constant, at around 60%, in all cases. Therefore, the optimum conditions were selected using fluorescence intensity as the sole criterion; the CDs resulting from a 2-min irradiation at 400 W yielded the highest intensity.

Fig. S2 of Supplementary material shows the FTIR spectrum of the synthesized CDs. As it can be seen, there is a broad band around 3320 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH groups, while the broad peak around 2920 cm−1 stands for the stretching vibrations of sp2 and sp3 CH [16]. The peak at 1040 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of COC, CO, C S and SO groups, while the peak at 1135 cm−1 can be ascribed to the CO, CN and CS bonds [17]. Finally, the peaks at 1590 and 1406 cm−1 confirm the existence of COO asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations [16].

The XRD pattern of CDs (Fig. S3 of Supplementary material) shows a wide diffraction peak located at ca. 22°, which corresponds to an interlayer spacing of about 0.404 nm and is indicative of the (002) interlayer spacing of graphitic structure [18]. Furthermore, TEM images revealed that CDs have circular shape with a diameter of around 2.2 nm and narrow size distribution (Fig. S4 of Supplementary material).

Fig. 2 a shows the deconvoluted XPS C1s peak of the as-synthesized CDs. The peak is analyzed into the following components: carbon–carbon bonds at 284.6 eV, sp2 and sp3 bonding configuration at 285.5 eV, carbon–oxygen bonds at 286.5 eV (epoxides and hyrdoxides) and 288.2 eV (carbonyls) [19]. A contribution from the sample holder is also present. Fig. 2b exhibits the XPS O1s peak deconvoluted into two components assigned to single oxygen–carbon bonds CO(H) or OCO at 532.6 eV and double oxygen–carbon bonds CO(H) or OCO(H) at 531.6 eV. The N1 s spectrum of Fig. 2c shows a peak centered at 399.6 eV, which is assigned to amine/amide or pyrolic moieties; a minor contribution of pyridinic nitrogen is present, as well. Finally, the S2p spectrum of Fig. 2d reveals a peak centered at a binding energy of 163.7 eV, which is assigned to sulfur–carbon bonds.

Using the total peak areas of O1s, C1s, S2p and N1s peaks and the appropriate sensitivity factors (based on Wagner’s collection and adjusted to the transmission characteristics of analyser EA10) and equations, the % average relative atomic concentrations were determined to be as follows: carbon 57.8%, oxygen 34.8%, nitrogen 5.9% and sulfur 1.5%.

The UV–vis spectrum of the aqueous CDs solution (Fig. S5 of Supplementary material) exhibits a broad peak around 300 nm, which is attributed to the n-π* transition of the carbonyl/amine functional groups [20]. Furthermore, the same solution exhibits a yellowish color under daylight conditions whereas a bright blue color appears, when excited at 360 nm (Fig. S5 of Supplementary material, inset).

The fluorescence emission spectra of a CDs solution were recorded at varying excitation wavelengths ranging between 250 and 350 nm. A strong emission band appeared at 380 nm, when excitation took place at 320 nm, as shown in Fig. 3 A. At this wavelength, n-π* transitions of high energy are mainly excited and as a result, emission occurs in the short wavelength region [21]. Excitation at higher wavelengths resulted in simultaneous red shift and decrease in the maximum fluorescence due to the heterogeneity of organic groups on the surface of CDs. These groups generate intermediate states between the HOMO and LUMO orbitals of the CDs, allowing emission over a wide range of the electromagnetic spectrum [22]. Additionally, a second, less intense emission peak was observed at excitation wavelengths lower than 270 nm. The dual emission of the CDs can be attributed to the doping with both nitrogen and sulfur. It is postulated that different surface states are created from nitrogen and sulfur resulting in different radiative recombination of electrons trapped in these surface states [23]. The excitation spectrum of Fig. 3B (monitored at an emission wavelength of 380 nm) shows that maximum fluorescence emission was achieved at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. Consequently, the combination of λex: 320 nm and λem: 380 nm was opted for as the optimum and finally employed for the subsequent experiments (this combination of λex and λem was found to be a trade-off between sensitivity for SY detection and selectivity).

The as-synthesized CDs exhibit strong photoluminescent properties, as the QY was calculated to be 42.8%. This high percentage suggests that the precursor and the microwave radiation method in combination with sulfuric acid are opportune for the production of CDs, besides being simple and cheap. Generally, CDs containing only C, O and H often lead to low photoluminescence quantum yields. For instance, Wang et al. prepared CDs by thermal decomposition of citric acid and the products were classified according to their degree of decomposition [24]. The thermally decomposed citric acid possessed either excitation-dependent or excitation-independent fluorescence as well as different quantum yields with varying synthesis conditions (i.e. temperature and reaction duration). Sahu et al. described the preparation of CDs using orange juice as a precursor, a non-toxic and easily accessible material, via a simple hydrothermal route and the resulting QY was reported to be 26% [25]. The as-synthesized CDs contained functional groups, such as COH, CC, CO, COC and CH on their surface and exhibited luminescence depending on the excitation wavelength, which was attributed to defects created in the structure generated by the functional groups. Lately, waste resources have been employed as precursors, for the synthesis of CDs, but still their QY remained low. For instance, Kumari et al. synthesized CDs from polyolefinic waste plastics, which exhibited fluorescence with QY of 4.84% [26]. In order to achieve higher quantum yields, doping CDs with nitrogen and/or sulfur is often employed. In this context, we have proposed a pyrolysis method from citrus species for nitrogen-doped QDs with QYs being of the highest found for CDs from low-cost natural sources [4]. However, just the presence of nitrogen or sulfur in the precursor does not warrant the production of high-quality CDs. On the contrary, molecules with the structure RHNCCSH (e.g. cysteine) tend to result in CDs with higher quantum yields [27], which is the case with the CDs herein.

A pivotal factor for the utilization of the CDs in diverse applications is their stability at various conditions. Since the pH is a main parameter for stability, the CDs were tested over the range of 2.0–12.0. The fluorescence intensity of CDs is pH-independent over a wide pH range of 3.0–10.0 (Fig. S6 of Supplementary material). More acidic or basic values cause a decrease in the fluorescence intensity. A reason may be that too many H+ or OH− ions induce a significant change in functional groups and then the electronic transition of some defects would be prohibited or disrupted [28]. The pH dependent properties at low pH, also, may be attributed to the aggregation of the synthesized CDs by their surrounding carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, resulting in fluorescence quenching.

In the context of stability study the ionic strength was tested by adding NaCl or Na2SO4 at various concentrations, up to 1.0 M. The photoluminescence was constant, despite the increase in the ionic strength with both salt types. This is indicative of good tolerance of CDs to salts, which is a key advantage in analytical applications. Continuous irradiation with a 150 W Xe lamp for 3 h showed high resistance of CDs to photobleaching (<2% decrease in the emitted fluorescence), under the above conditions. Additionally, fluorescence was scarcely reduced after 72 h of storage under ambient temperature conditions (less than 3%), while the CDs showed no signs of precipitation, indicating that the CDs possess acceptable optical and colloidal stability. Finally, lyophilized CDs stored for 3 months at room temperature, exhibited fluorescence intensity similar to that of freshly prepared CDs. On the basis of the above results, the synthesized CDs exhibit excellent photostability, stability over a wide range of pH and ionic strength, facilitating their applicability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent probing of sunset yellow and selectivity study
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the aforementioned merits, the synthesized CDs were utilized to develop an analytical method whereby SY was able to quench the emitted fluorescence of the CDs. Typically, 34 nM of SY cause nearly 50% fluorescence quenching, accompanied by a minor blue-shift of about 6 nm at the emission wavelength of the CDs (Fig. S7 of Supplementary material), entailing that the fluorophores of the CDs are placed in a more hydrophobic microenvironment in the presence of SY [29]. Therefore, a sensitive fluorimetric assay for SY was developed based on the quenching from the interaction between the analyte and the CDs. Before establishing the analytical method, the optimum experimental conditions were examined in order to maximize the probing potential of the method. The pH of the solution was examined in the range of 3.0-11.0, since the fluorescence was constant over this range, as already mentioned and the results showed a pH-independent quenching of the fluorescence, in the presence of SY (data not shown). It can be inferred that the proposed method can be used without any pH adjustment.

The second parameter examined was the role of ionic strength in the fluorescent detection system. Even at the highest concentration tested (1.0 M of NaCl or Na2SO4) no alterations were observed in the fluorescence quenching, making it possible for CDs to be applied to food matrices with high salt content. Finally, the necessary time for the reaction to reach equilibrium was tested. The interaction between SY and CDs, which is responsible for the fluorescence quenching is instant, resulting in a fast method.

Prior to testing the applicability of the developed method, experiments were conducted to ensure the selectivity towards SY. The probing effect was expressed as the quenching of the CDs fluorescence emission caused by other compounds commonly found in food products. The quenching of the CDs fluorescence in the presence of 70 nM of SY or 210 nM of various compounds can be seen in Fig. S8 of Supplementary material. Evidently, the synthesized CDs are selective for SY over components commonly found in food sample matrixes and metal ions like iron and mercury, which are ordinary quenchers of such CDs. It is noteworthy that tartrazine, which is another azo-dye widely used in food products did not cause any fluorescence quenching, highlighting the selectivity of CDs for SY.

Under optimum conditions, the analytical performance of the developed method was evaluated. The developed method shows good linearity (Fig. 4 ) at SY concentrations in the range of 0.3–43.4 nM, with the correlation coefficient to be 0.9958. The limits of detection (signal-to-noise ratio = 3) and quantification (S/N = 10) of the method are 0.1 nM and 0.3 nM, respectively. The intra-day (reproducibility) and inter-day (repeatability) relative standard deviations are 2.5% and 3.1% (three measurements), respectively.

The applicability of the method was examined by performing standard recovery experiments in ice-cream, juice and energy drink (Table 1 ). The recoveries of SY were satisfactory, ranging between 96.7 and 99.7%, 96.7 and 99.0% and 95.0 and 98.0% in ice-cream, juice and energy drink, respectively. Obviously, the various constituents of the tested foodstuffs cause no or negligible interference to the detection of SY, reinforcing the suitability of the developed method for food samples. Overall, it can be concluded that the proposed method for SY probing has negligible cost, selectivity, sensitivity and shows linear response at low concentrations, while it is more sensitive and faster compared to others previously reported (Table S1 of Supplementary material).

Fluorescence quenching can occur via different mechanisms of interaction (e.g. static or dynamic quenching, inner-filter effect etc.) between the fluorophore (i.e. CDs) and the quencher (i.e. SY) [30,6]. The plots of F 0/F (F 0 and F denote the fluorescence values in the absence and presence of SY, respectively) versus the concentration of SY were drawn at temperatures of 25, 35 and 45 °C (Fig. S9 of Supplementary material). As temperature increases, the slope decreases along with the Stern-Volmer constants of CDs-SY, indicating that static quenching dominates [30]. Fig. S10 of Supplementary material shows the individual UV spectra of CDs, SY, of their mixture, as well as the sum of the first two spectra. Apparently, the absorbance of the mixture CDs-SY is lower than the theoretically calculated summed value, strengthening the notion of the static quenching mechanism [30].

Aside from the ground-state complex formation in the static quenching mechanism, a substantial overlap between the fluorescence emission spectrum of the fluorophore and the UV absorption spectrum of the quencher (Fig. S11 of Supplementary material) may imply the presence of an additional mechanism. The binding distance r concerning the energy donor (fluorophore) and the energy acceptor (quencher) was found to be 14.8 nm (see Section 6 of Supplementary material) and hence, the primary condition for Förster non-radiation energy transfer process (<8 nm) is not fulfilled [30]. Although the mechanism of inner filter effect differs from the above mentioned, it can reasonably be exploited for analytical purposes [31]. The spectra of SY at various concentrations in the presence of the CDs (Fig. S10 (inset) of Supplementary material), revealed that at the excitation and emission wavelengths of CDs, the molecular absorption of SY increased significantly as the concentration of SY increased. Finally, by alternating the light path at excitation and emission, the results demonstrated (data not shown) that inner filter effect occurred both at excitation and emission wavelength. More pronounced was the effect at the excitation wavelength. Hence, the observed quenching could not be attributed exclusively to the static quenching mechanism.

To assess the real magnitude of the static quenching mechanism, corrections for the inner filter effect were carried out at all tested concentrations of SY, as described in Section 6 of Supplementary material. Using the corrected values, the fitted Stern-Volmer plot was drawn and the upward curvature, initially observed, then was absent (Fig. S12 of Supplementary material). Therefore, the static quenching mechanism was operative and a non-fluorescent complex in the ground state was formed. The overall, observed fluorescence quenching of the CDs from SY is attributed both to static mechanism and inner filter effect.

Using the corrected values, the binding constant, Kb and the Hill coefficient, n were also calculated from the following equation (1):
(1)
l o g
F 0
− F
F
= l o g
K b
+ n l o g
[ Q ]
where [Q] is the free concentration of SY [32]. The calculations yielded a Kb of 2.3 × 104 M−1 (Fig. S13 of Supplementary material), indicating a strong binding between CDs and SY and corroborating the static quenching mechanism in addition to inner filter effect. The n value was found to be close to the unit, bespeaking the presence of one kind, high-affinity binding site on the CDs for SY.

Potential binding between CDs and SY may arise from different interactions, such as (a) hydrogen bonding due to the presence of sulfonate, azo and hydroxyl group on the molecule of SY, (b) Van der Waals forces by means of permanent or induced dipoles and (c) π−π stacking because of the aromatic rings in both molecules and the abundance of π electrons. This, latter type of interaction is also supported by the dominance of azo tautomer of SY at the working pH range over hydrazine tautomer, which occurs at low pH values, since more π electrons (i.e. bigger flat core) are available for interactions [33]. In addition to above, the incorporation of sulfur in CDs, assists in the tuning of the electron density of the CDs towards the (phenolic) hydroxyl groups, due to the electron donating ability of sulfur and the electron-withdrawing ability of oxygen [34]. As a result, the aromatic rings have higher electron deficiency, compared to those of non-doped CDs and are more prone to participating in anion-π interactions [34,35]. Since SY molecule has two sulfonate anion groups, it is highly anticipated that anion-π interactions between CDs and SY may also arise. Such interactions can lead to an increase in the aromaticity of the rings and to disturbances of the sp2 clusters of the aromatic rings, which have been correlated with blue fluorescence of CDs [35,36].

The viability of HeLa, A549, MDA-MB-231 and HEK-293 cells was examined after their exposure to CDs up to 200 μg mL−1. The cell viability was approximately 98% after 48 h of incubation, even at the highest tested concentration (200 μg mL−1). No significant difference between the CDs-treated samples and control was observed by Student's t-test (p < 0.01), demonstrating that the CDs do not induce in vitro toxicity, after incubation for 48 h (Fig. S14 of Supplementary material). Since the exposure times tested were much longer than those usually employed for bioimaging applications, it can safely be deduced that CDs are biocompatible and suitable for cellular imaging.

The CDs possess distinctive advantages marked by their small size, low cytotoxicity, prominent biocompatibility and excellent photostability, which renders them ideal candidate for fluorescence imaging [37]. To examine the potential of CDs as cell imaging probes, cells were incubated with the CDs and observed under a fluorescence microscope. As shown as in Fig. 5 , all cells are lightened, confirming the high cellular uptake of the CDs and the high distribution of CDs in cell cytoplasm. Also, the bright-field optical images validated the absence of morphological differences between CDs-treated cells and non-treated ones. Therefore, it can be concluded that the prepared CDs from human fingernails are suitable for cell imaging.

Natural organic sources have drawn significant attention to CDs synthesis, due to the abundance of carbon, varieties of heteroatom doping (such as N, S, P), low toxicity and good biocompatibility as precursors. This is the first time that human fingernails are used in a synthesis and more specifically for that of N,S co-doped CDs. This strategy not only avoids the use of toxic or expensive solvents and starting materials but also provides an effective way to reclaim nail wastes. The employed method is energy-efficient and fast. The clean-up of the as-synthesized CDs is achieved by a solid-phase extraction step, cutting down the time needed for purification. In contrast to many reported methods, no surface passivation agents and passivation steps are incorporated in the synthesis route. The as-synthesized bright CDs can be used for the highly sensitive, low-cost, fast and interference-free detection of SY in food products. Moreover, the CDs induce no cell mortality and can effectively be used as bioimaging agents.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Many thanks are due to the XRD and TEM center of University of Ioannina for obtaining the XRD spectra and TEM images.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.04.059.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Full-color light-emitting carbon dots with a surface-state-controlled luminescence mechanism
Carbon dots-based fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective detection of iodide
Two of a kind but different: luminescent carbon quantum dots from Citrus peels for iron and tartrazine sensing and cell imaging
Selective FRET-based sensing of 4-nitrophenol and cell imaging capitalizing on the fluorescent properties of carbon nanodots from apple seeds
The quenching of the fluorescence of carbon dots: a review on mechanisms and applications
An efficient fluorescent probe for fluazinam using N, S co-doped carbon dots from l-cysteine
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots: a facile and green fluorescence probe for free chlorine
A facile hydrothermal approach towards photoluminescent carbon dots from amino acids
Green synthesis of bifunctional fluorescent carbon dots from garlic for cellular imaging and free radical scavenging
Hair fiber as a precursor for synthesizing of sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots with tunable luminescence properties
Electrochemical determination of food colorants in soft drinks using MWCNT-modified GCEs
Facile synthesis of Au supported on ionic liquid functionalized reduced graphene oxide for simultaneous determination of Sunset yellow and Tartrazine in drinks
Cu-BTC frameworks-based electrochemical sensing platform for rapid and simple determination of Sunset yellow and Tartrazine
One-pot synthesis of carbon dots using two different acids and their respective unique photoluminescence property
Green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots from lotus root for Hg(II) ions detection and cell imaging
High fluorescence S, N co-doped carbon dots as an ultra-sensitive fluorescent probe for the determination of uric acid
Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots with excellent thermal and photo stability applied as invisible ink for loading important information and anti-counterfeiting
Study of the thermal reduction of graphene oxide and of its application as electrocatalyst in quasi-solid state dye-sensitized solar cells in combination with PEDOT
Formation of N, S-codoped fluorescent carbon dots from biomass and their application for the selective detection of mercury and iron ion
Spectrochim. Acta A: Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.
Molecular fluorescence in citric acid-based carbon dots
Engineering surface states of carbon dots to achieve controllable luminescence for solid-luminescent composites and sensitive Be2+ detection
Comparative study for N and S doped carbon dots: synthesis, characterization and applications for Fe3+ probe and cellular imaging
Structural evolution of graphene quantum dots during thermal decomposition of citric acid and the corresponding photoluminescence
Simple one-step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from orange juice: application as excellent bio-imaging agents
Synthesis of green fluorescent carbon quantum dots using waste polyolefins residue for Cu2+ ion sensing and live cell imaging
Rational design of high quality citric acid-derived carbon dots by selecting efficient chemical structure motifs
Easy synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots from gelatin and their luminescent properties and applications
Characterizing the interaction between tartrazine and two serum albumins by a hybrid spectroscopic approach
Correction of inner-filter effect in fluorescence excitation-emission matrix spectrometry using Raman scatter
Fluorescence quenching to study protein-ligand binding: common errors
Concentration, temperature, and pH dependence of sunset-yellow aggregates in aqueous solutions: an x-ray investigation
Sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots as a novel fluorescent probe for highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+
Exciton dynamics in luminescent carbon nanodots: electron–hole exchange interaction
Carbon dots—emerging light emitters for bioimaging, cancer therapy and optoelectronics

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Prof. Constantine Stalikas received his Bachelor and PhD degree from the Department of Chemistry of University of Ioannina, in 1988 and 1994, respectively. He joined the Department of Chemistry of University of Cordoba, Spain, in 1997 and the Institute of Hydrochemistry of Technical University of Munich, Germany, in 2000. In the same year, he became a lecturer. His current research interests focus on the development of analytical methods using carbon quantum dots from natural sources, as sensing probes.

Prof. Anastasios Troganis received his Bachelor and PhD degree from the Department of Chemistry of University of Athens, in 1980 and 1992, respectively. In 1993 he joined the Department of Chemistry, Ohio State University, at Ohio, USA (ΝΙΗ grant), as postdoctoral associate. In 2003, he became lecturer in the Department of Biological Applications and Technology of University of Ioannina. His current research interests focus on the analysis of complex natural products of biological interest and spectroscopic study of molecular structure of biologically active compounds.

Prof. Ioannis Leonardos received his Bachelor and PhD degree from the Department of Biology of Aristotle University of Thessaloniki, in 1981 and 1996, respectively. He has obtained long experience working as research associate in ecology of aquatic organisms and ecotoxicology. In 2001, he became assistant professor in the Department of Biological Applications and Technology of University of Ioannina. His current research interests focus on the ecotoxicology and on the impact of carbon quantum dots on living organisms (zebrafish).

Dr. Sygellou Lamprini received her Bachelor degree from the Department of Physics of University of Ioannina, in 1999. Then, she joined the Department of Chemical Engineering of University of Patras, Greece, where she received her MSc and PhD degree, in 2005. Now she is member of the Composites and Nanomaterials Laboratory the of Institute of Chemical Engineering Sciences (ICE-HT) and her interests focus on the characterization of materials with surface science techniques and the preparation and characterization of graphene-like substrates for the study of their electronic, optical and structural properties.

Theodoros Chatzimitakos received his Bachelor and MSc degree from the Department of Chemistry of University of Ioannina, Greece, in 2013 and 2015, respectively. He is currently pursuing a PhD in Analytical Chemistry, at Chemistry Department of University of Ioannina. His main scientific interest is the development of (bio)analytical methods based on nanomaterials.

Athanasia Kasouni received her Bachelor degree from the Department of Biological Applications and Technology, of University of Ioannina, Greece, in 2014. Currently, she is pursuing a PhD degree in the same Department. Her scientific interests include the cytotoxicity assessment of carbon-based nanomaterials and their utilization in biological applications.
